title: RA.992 Laurel Halo

translation.content: Music's therapeutic value is often linked to relaxation–gongs, singing bowls and the like. Dense passages of foggy droning and eerie static aren't traditionally considered restorative but Laurel Halo makes a pretty good case for it. The Detroit-born, Los Angeles-based musician's abstract, often improvised, productions are heavy on sound design and emotional climax. Driven by atmosphere rather than rhythm, they push listeners to grapple with their innermost insecurities, fears and dreams. "I'm lucky my music has helped people through crises," Halo once told Discwoman. It's easy to see why. Since her 2010 debut King Felix, she has built a stunningly diverse catalogue of classically-informed records. A multi-instrumentalist—piano, violin, guitar, keys—her sharpest instrument is arrangement. Inspired by the surrealism of Italo Calvino and Apichatpong Weerasethakul, her releases, from Atlas to Behind The Green Door, unfold with slow-burning narrative and dense emotional weight. Her soundworlds are layered and labyrinthine—an architectonic space where self-reflection happens almost by force. Even in the club, the sought-after composer excels in immersion. Her sets extend the expressionist palette of her records, trading traditional rhythm for tension, space and surprise. It's no wonder she takes a genre-agnostic approach to the dance floor, with deep roots in freeform radio, beginning at her time with WCBN-FM in Michigan, followed by stint on Berlin Community Radio, Rinse FM and now a regular show on NTS. RA.992 plays like a shadowy extension of that instinct: foggy ambient loops, propulsive techno, mutant percussion and heady left turns, stitched with care but never smoothed into predictability. Tracks from DJ Rush, Octave One and Eddie Fowlkes nod to her Midwestern heritage, balanced out by deeper, psychedelic fare from the likes of Polygonia and Cousin. It's the mark of an artist revealing both deep curiosity and a precise hand as a selector. Rare, indeed. – Nyshka Chandran What have you been up to recently? In my day to day, just swimming a lot of laps at the Y, working in the studio, and reading. The past couple months I composed the score for this beautiful underwater film by the artist Julian Charrière called Midnight Zone, which was a dream project to have worked on. The visual narrative is hypnotic, meditative and pressurized, following a submerged light as it encounters sea life while descending into the abyss. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded in my home studio in LA. The idea behind the mix was just to play some of my favorite tracks in my DJ sets from the past nine months up until now. It's mostly grooves: dub or acid-leaning percussive workouts, with deep harmonies, sound design and raw drums. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm looking forward to being back in Europe this month, doing some DJ gigs and seeing friends in various places. While I'm there, I'll attend the premiere of the film I mentioned—it's part of a larger solo show at the Tinguely Museum in Basel. I'm looking forward to hearing and seeing the work in person. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I would maybe have to zoom out a little and say more than a specific club or party that sound systems and the encounter or living inside of amplified sound in general was the most impactful part of the club music experience. Knowing what frequencies feel like coursing through one's body, and how healing the forces of rhythm and sound can be. Going to DEMF and then Movement with some orchestra friends starting from when I was 15 set me on the path because I had known the euphoric feeling of collective acoustic sound by that point, but not yet that of collective amplified sound. Later, when I lived in Berlin, I got a lot out of going to Wax Treatment. Aba Shanti-I at Notting Hill Carnival also remains a cherished memory. You've spoken before about letting intuition guide your selections. What does intuitive DJing look like to you—are you feeling the room, chasing a mood or something else entirely? It's all three I guess. On the one hand it's just the simple act of reading the room, 'reading the air,' detecting the unspoken mood and social cues in a situation. At the same time, it's fun to ride the energy, maybe for example punishing for a few minutes with something serious, hard or unhinged (for example "Body" by Wata Igarashi) and then releasing with a switch into something more euphoric, sexy or humorous. I probably also bring some pitch awareness into the mix and intuitively select by how well tracks blend harmonically with one another. So I guess being tapped into the present, flexible and perceptive, respecting and challenging at the same time all go into it. How does the context of a club mix shift your approach compared to radio? They're related, but again context-dependent. I often listen to NTS at home by myself or with a couple other friends, or when I'm wandering around. It’s more about creating an atmosphere or mood for a private versus a public or collective sphere. I think with both I'm aiming for some kind of healing or confidence-building, attempting to feel freer or more present in one's headspace or body. How does your relationship with the club—both as a space and an idea—inform your productions, and vice versa? As mentioned before, the physical soundsystem experience was and is deeply impactful to me. Experiencing the full range and power of amplified sound taught me simple rules about EQing and compression, as well as how empty or dense one's tracks can be. It's natural that DJing leads both to better ears and hearing loss. As an idea also, creating music that is inviting, enveloping, engrossing or sublimating, that's the inspiring aspect of playing club music or going out dancing. What kinds of club music are you most drawn to right now, either as a DJ or a listener? Right now I'm obsessed with empty, psychedelic, percussive tools, anything with a polyrhythmic or groovy pulse. They can be so useful for switching genres, moods or eras, and also simply fun to dance to. I also love the sound of real drum samples or unquantized rhythms blended with synthetic sounds or hard-clocked rhythms. I guess I just like a lot of strains of acid too, it can tap into all my favourite affects, whether serious or punishing, euphoric or joyful, wormhole or dissociative, or just simply goofy.
translation.blurb: A rare club mix from the ever-evolving artist, with 90 minutes of shadowy, atmospheric pressure.
